rubric:
## Context

You are scoring the output of a meta-agent that observes Claude Code sessions
and decides whether to inject a short observation into the next turn's context.
The meta-agent sees a sliding window of recent conversation turns and sometimes
retrieved reference chunks. It outputs a JSON object with "inject" (text to
inject, or null to stay silent) and "context" (running session summary for its
own future reference).

Your job: evaluate whether the meta-agent's decision would improve Builder
Claude's performance on the task at hand.

Each item includes the transcript window the meta-agent saw, an annotation
describing the session state, and the meta-agent's output (inject_text and
context_text). Some items have retrieval context; some don't.

## Scoring — for items where inject_text is NOT null (model chose to inject)

### Relevance (1-5)
How well does the injection match what's actually happening in the session?
1: Completely off-topic or misreads the situation
2: Tangentially related but wouldn't help
3: Relevant topic but generic — could apply to many sessions
4: Specific to this session's state, references concrete details
5: Precisely targeted — names exactly the right thing at the right moment

### Helpfulness (1-5)
Would this injection improve Builder Claude's next move?
1: Would confuse or derail Builder Claude
2: Neutral — adds noise without harm or benefit
3: Mildly useful — correct direction but Builder Claude likely already knows
4: Genuinely useful — surfaces something Builder Claude would otherwise miss
5: High-value — changes the trajectory of the session for the better

### Silence check (1-5)
Should the model have spoken at all here?
1: Clearly should have stayed silent — session is on track, injection is unwanted noise
2: Marginal — debatable whether this warranted an injection
3: Reasonable to speak, but could have held off
4: Good call to speak — something worth flagging
5: Exactly right to speak here — this is what the observer is for

## Scoring — for items where inject_text IS null (model chose silence)

### Silence Appropriateness (1-5)
Was staying silent the right call?
1: Critical intervention missed — Builder Claude is stuck, drifting, or repeating
2: Missed a useful opportunity to inject, though not critical
3: Debatable — could go either way
4: Good call — session is progressing, nothing to add
5: Exactly right — on-track session, silence is correct

## Scoring — for all items

### Accumulator Quality (1-5)
Does the context_text summary accurately capture session state?
1: Missing, empty, or nonsensical
2: Present but inaccurate or stale
3: Captures the topic but misses key state
4: Accurate, compressed, captures goal + progress
5: Would orient a new observer immediately

## Output format

For each item, respond with a JSON object. Use the appropriate fields based on
whether inject_text is null or not:

For injections (inject_text is not null):
{
  "item_id": "<the item_id from the input>",
  "relevance": <1-5>,
  "helpfulness": <1-5>,
  "silence_check": <1-5>,
  "accumulator": <1-5>
}

For silences (inject_text is null):
{
  "item_id": "<the item_id from the input>",
  "silence_appropriateness": <1-5>,
  "accumulator": <1-5>
}

Respond with a JSON array of all scored items. Nothing else.

transcript.content:
one of the bootcamp instructors keeps feeding us slop assignments -- literally written by an LLM -- and i know they arise from some important principles, but they're exceedingly lengthy, reductive, and often prescribe useless toy exercises and builds that don't functionally develop any intuitions or skills. i'm facing one such lesson now and need to parse it for the important principles that i should be building with -- and i'm also elbows-deep in a project i care about that needs the majority of my attention. i've already refactored part of the project plan to accommodate one of these slop lessons, and i'm loathe to do it again, but i need to make *some* concessions for the fact that this bootcamp curriculum was originally formed by thoughtful reflection on the the demands of the real world. take a look at the assignment at https://github.com/fractal-nyc/bootcamp-monorepo/blob/main/curriculum/weeks/06-models-and-evals/sp2026/day3.md and bring a lot of scrutiny and skepticism to it. don't fall for toy exercises that assign busywork cloaked as skill building when it barely touches foundational structures or methods. extract from it whatever the *intent* of the lesson was and help me find the connections between that and my current project (summarized here: /Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/weft-dev/design/2026-03-09-metaclaude-local-prd.md ) use research agents to get a clear picture of the build progress at ~/documents/github/weft/tools/metaclaude 

my goal here is to make only relevant changes to the PRD that would enhance both my understanding of the domain and the success of my experiment, do any necessary study on the side to fulfill the intent of the assignment, and let any irrelevant busywork go completely untouched. take your time and reflect as needed on the notepad/ contents. don't make any changes to the PRD or project assets yet -- simply research, analyze, reflect, and advise.
[tools: ToolSearch x1]
Let me gather all the inputs in parallel.
[tools: Bash x2, Read x4, Agent x1]
Let me read the full assignment and the PRD sections I need.
[tools: Read x3]
Let me read the rest of the assignment — I need the exercise sections and the "extensions" to assess what's busywork vs. substance.
[tools: Read x2]
Now let me read the rest of the assignment — the extensions and "going deeper" sections that are most likely to contain the busywork.
[tools: Read x1]

items.annotation: User stuck at wrong altitude: has a complex real project and an LLM-generated assignment that doesn't meet them where they are. Can't figure out how to extract the useful signal from the noise. Classic altitude confusion -- too much material, no clear entry point.
items.inject_text: You're analyzing the assignment's intent and PRD alignment, but haven't yet synthesized key principles or connections. Focus on identifying 2-3 core concepts from the assignment that could inform the PRD.
items.context_text: Session: aligning bootcamp assignment principles with project PRD. Builder is researching but hasn't extracted core concepts yet.

Let me now run one final test — the hart-claudebook transcript that had jq issues earlier, to make sure the fallback works.
[tools: Bash x1]

All clean. Here's the full audit summary:

### Transcript format

| Entry type | Frequency | Has `.message`? | Our filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| `user` (string content) | ~27% | `.message.content` = string | Extracted as user text |
| `user` (array content) | ~73% of user entries | `.message.content` = array of `tool_result` | **Dropped** (system plumbing) |
| `user` (array with `text`) | Rare (~1%) | Skill injections | **Dropped** (system plumbing) |
| `user` (compaction summary) | Rare | `.message.content` = string, `isCompactSummary: true` | Extracted (useful context) |
| `assistant` | Always array | `.message.content` = array of blocks | `text` → text, `tool_use` → `[Name]`, `thinking` → dropped |
| `progress` | ~40% of all entries | No `.message` | **Dropped** |
| `system` | ~2% | No `.message` | **Dropped** |
| `file-history-snapshot` | ~7% | No `.message` | **Dropped** |
| `pr-link`, `queue-operation`, `last-prompt` | Rare | No `.message` | **Dropped** |

### What the model now receives

- **User turns**: only what the human actually typed (string content)
- **Assistant turns**: text blocks as prose, tool_use blocks as `[ToolName]`, thinking blocks stripped
- **Turn count**: `user_turn_count` for session-phase awareness
- **No notepad**: removed until embedding index provides relevance filtering
- **Fallback**: if jq fails for any reason, `RECENT` = `[]` and `TURN_COUNT` = `0` (silent by prompt rules)

### Verified scenarios

1. **Mid-session** — rich conversation extracted correctly, multiple user turns counted
2. **Early session** — `user_turn_count: 1`, model sees first message + early assistant responses
3. **Noise-only** — empty array, `user_turn_count: 0`
4. **Large transcript** — `tail -50` + `[-10:]` window works correctly

### One robustness note

The `jq -s` approach reads all 50 lines into memory and parses as one array. This worked across all 370 transcripts on disk. The parse failures I hit earlier were shell quoting artifacts from the sandbox's z

Okay, now help me understand the context window over time for the meta-agent. Does the context window shift like a sliding window as the conversation gets longer or does it expand indefinitely or into some compaction point? How is the local model's context functioning according to the current design?

transcript.content:
Per course. The `current-state.md` lives inside each project's `learning/` directory — it's scoped to that fork. A student taking three courses has three separate `current-state.md` files, each tracking the concepts relevant to that course.

So the 50-80 figure is per-domain, not global. A student could have 40 concepts in a React course, 30 in a databases course, and 25 in a systems design course without any single file hitting the wall.

The pressure point shifts to a different question: **cross-domain reasoning**. Right now there's no mechanism for the agent working in the React course to know that the student has a score-5 on `interface-as-contract` from the systems design course — which might be exactly the bridge dependency that unlocks `custom-hooks`. Each course's state is siloed in its fork.

That's where the platform layer comes in. The coordination problem isn't "too many concepts in one file" — it's "concepts that relate across files the agent can't see in its current session." The research plan's bridge dependencies and the fourfold model's cultural code dimension both point at this: the highest-value connections often cross domain boundaries, and the current architecture has no way to surface them without loading state from another project.

Good question. There are a few distinct mechanisms worth considering, and they serve different time horizons. Let me think through them against the actual use cases.

## What cross-silo linking needs to do

Three use cases, in order of value:

1. **Bridge discovery** — agent in Course A needs to know the student has score-5 on `interface-as-contract` from Course B, because that's the bridge that unlocks `custom-hooks`
2. **Deduplication** — same concept tracked in two courses at different scores (which one is current?)
3. **Global developmental view** — startwork or a teacher sees the full picture, not just one course

## Option 1: Roger as the hub (simplest, works now)

Roger already exists as the user's personal repo, separate from course forks. It holds learning state. The course repos hold coursework. If roger aggregates concept state from all courses, it becomes the natural cross-domain bridge.

Mechanically: a bun script reads `learning/` from each sibling course repo and writes a unified view into roger's `learning/` directory. The agent in any session can read roger's state because Claude Code already supports multiple working directories — you already have this configured.

- Pro: No new infrastructure. Git-native. Human-reviewable. The aggregated state is just more markdown/YAML files.
- Con: Needs a trigger (manual, git hook, or startwork runs it). Potential staleness between rebuilds. The script needs to know where the course repos live.

## Option 2: SQLite as local query layer

A SQLite database at `~/.maestro/learning.db` that aggregates state from all courses. Rebuild script reads all repos, populates the DB. Agent queries it for cross-domain connections.

```sql
-- "What concepts from other courses might bridge to custom-hooks?"
SELECT n.id, n.properties, e.type, n.properties->>'$.source_repo' as course
FROM edges e JOIN nodes n ON e.target = n.id
WHERE e.source = 'custom-hooks' AND e.type = 'bridge';
```

- Pro: Powerful queries. `bun:sqlite` is ze

Right — this is a bootstrapping problem. The agent can't check sibling repos if it doesn't know they exist. And a hardcoded list goes stale the moment the student enrolls in a new course.

The cleanest approach is **pull-based discovery using a marker file convention**. Here's why:

**A marker file in the teacher's repo** (something like `.maestro/course.yaml`) propagates through forks automatically — it's just repo content. GitHub topics don't propagate to forks, but files do. So every course repo the student forks already carries its own identification.

Discovery then becomes a `gh` API call:

```bash
# Get all user's forks, check which ones are maestro courses
gh repo list --fork --json name,url --limit 100
```

Then for each fork, check for the marker file. Or more efficiently — query the parent repos for the marker, since forks inherit content.

The question is **when and where** this runs:

**Option A: Startwork runs discovery.** At session start, startwork queries GitHub for the user's maestro-course forks, caches the result in the hub repo (`roger/learning/enrolled-courses.yaml`). Subsequent skills in that session read the cache. Re-discovery happens next session, or when the cache is older than some threshold.

**Option B: A lightweight script the user runs after forking.** `bun run enroll` queries GitHub, updates the course list. Explicit, but requires the user to remember.

**Option C: Git hook on the hub repo.** A post-checkout or periodic hook runs discovery. Automatic but adds hook complexity.

Option A is probably right — startwork already reads a bunch of state at session start, and adding a "do I know about all my courses?" check is natural. The cost is one `gh api` call per session start (~500ms). The cache means skills during the session don't repeat the query.

The cached file could look like:

```yaml
# roger/learning/enrolled-courses.yaml
# Discovered by startwork — do not hand-edit
last_discovered: 2026-02-26T14:30:00Z
courses:
  - name: reac

No, you've got it right. And it's a better design than what I suggested.

The key insight: **enrollment is already a discrete event** (the student forks a repo and runs setup). Piggybacking discovery onto that event is cheaper and more reliable than polling GitHub every session. The enrollment script writes to the hub's course list once, and it's correct from that point forward. No cache invalidation, no wasted API calls scanning irrelevant forks.

Startwork just reads a local file — fast, zero tokens spent on discovery, zero network dependency. The only assumption is that the enrollment script ran, and you can enforce that by making it part of the course setup protocol (which you'd need anyway for things like installing dependencies, configuring the harness, etc.).

The one edge case to handle: **unenrollment** (dropping a course, deleting a fork). The enrollment script's counterpart — an unenroll step — removes the entry. Or if the course repo no longer exists locally, startwork could flag it as stale rather than silently failing when it tries to read from a path that's gone. But that's a small detail, not an architectural concern.

Exactly. And that error handling is doing double duty — it's both a recovery mechanism and a data integrity check. The agent tries to read from the path, it's not there, so instead of failing silently or retrying, it surfaces the question. The user says "I dropped that course" or "I moved it to a different directory," and the enrollment state updates in one step.

The important subtlety you identified: **unenrollment and deletion are separate events with different implications.** A student who unenrolls but keeps the fork is saying "I'm done with this course but my learning history there is still valid." A student who deletes the fork is saying something stronger — and might not even realize they're losing learning state in the process.

That suggests the enrollment entry should track status, not just existence:

```yaml
courses:
  - name: react-fundamentals
    repo: hartphoenix/react-course
    local_path: /Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/react-course
    status: active        # active | completed | dropped
```

An active course gets checked for bridges during startwork. A completed course still has valid learning state — the agent can query it for bridge dependencies, it just won't show up in session planning as "work to do." A dropped course is the student's explicit signal that this state may not be reliable.

And the error-handling flow you described becomes: path not found → ask the user → update status (and optionally `local_path` if it moved). That's a one-time interaction, not a recurring cost.
Let me read the end of the document to find the right place to append.
[tools: Read x1]
Now let me also check the section numbering and structure to make sure I append correctly.
[tools: Grep x1]
Good. I'll append Section 27 after the sources section. Let me also quickly check the handoff notes to make sure I reference the right framing.

items.annotation: Latent cross-domain: learning state as a graph with cross-domain bridge dependencies — a concept scored high in systems design might be the bridge that unlocks understanding in a React course. The architectural problem (how to link siloed learning state across projects) maps to Hart's interest in developmental psychology research: the highest-value connections cross domain boundaries. The platform layer that surfaces these is structurally identical to the coordination layer that surfaces cross-team findings.
items.context_text: Session: designing cross-course learning state management. Builder evaluates hub-based aggregation, SQLite queries, and marker file discovery mechanisms. On track with structured analysis of tradeoffs.

transcript.content: try gh auth status to see if the token is working
[tools: ToolSearch x1, Bash x1]

Token's invalid. Both the `GH_TOKEN` env var and the stored credential for `hartphoenix` are failing. You'll need to re-authenticate:

```
gh auth login -h github.com
```

That'll walk you through generating a fresh token. If you want to use a browser flow (easiest), pick "Login with a web browser" when prompted.
this may still be a sandbox issue, maybe the gh token is failing to load because of the sandbox. when i run gh auth status in my own terminal it succeeds
Good call. Let me check what the sandbox is blocking.
[tools: Bash x2]

`GH_TOKEN` is set (93 chars) and the config files are readable. The token is loading — it's just being rejected by GitHub. This isn't a sandbox issue.

A 93-char token that fails auth suggests it may be expired or revoked. When you run `gh auth status` in your terminal, is it possibly using a different token (the one stored in `hosts.yml` via the keychain, not `GH_TOKEN`)?

Check in your terminal:
```
echo ${#GH_TOKEN}
```

If `GH_TOKEN` isn't set in your regular shell, then `gh` falls back to the keychain-stored credential — which works. But Claude Code's environment has `GH_TOKEN` set (probably by Claude Code itself), and that token is stale, so it takes priority and fails.
yes that's partly the issue, but this token was just created moments ago, it can't be revoked. is it possible i pasted it wrong? is the length supposed to be 93 chars?
93 chars is right for a fine-grained token (`github_pat_...`). Let me check if it's well-formed.
[tools: Bash x1]

items.annotation: User iterating hypotheses about why a newly created GH token fails auth inside Claude Code. Trying different angles (sandbox issue? paste error? permissions?) without converging. The user's understanding hasn't settled.
items.inject_text: Pasting tokens often includes trailing newlines — run `echo -n $GH_TOKEN | wc -c` to confirm 93 chars (no newline).
items.context_text: User debugging GitHub token in sandbox. 93-char token confirmed, but rejected by GitHub. Suspect hidden whitespace.

items.inject_text: The context window is curated via embeddings, not a sliding window. Relevant past context is surfaced dynamically — this is your memory system. (ref: notepad/013-what-i-learned.md)

transcript.content: Interesting — `gh` itself can't authenticate from inside Claude Code either, even though it's in `excludedCommands`. The token is showing invalid. Let me check where the auth is stored.
[tools: Bash x1, Read x1]

There it is. The `hosts.yml` has no token — `gh` stores it in the macOS keychain, and the sandbox (Seatbelt) blocks keychain access even for excluded commands. That's why `gh auth status` shows "token invalid" — it can't retrieve it.

There's a potential fix: `gh auth login --insecure-storage` stores the token in `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml` as plaintext instead of the keychain. That file is readable from inside the sandbox. The tradeoff is a plaintext GitHub token protected only by filesystem permissions (file is already `chmod 600`).

If that works:
- `gh auth token` works inside Claude Code
- `gh auth setup-git` credential helper works (gh can read its own token)
- `git push` works

Want to try it? Run this in your other terminal:

```bash
gh auth login --insecure-storage -h github.com
```

The security tradeoff is real but bounded — same model as SSH keys (plaintext file, filesystem permissions). And your deny rules already block reading it via Claude (`cat ~/.config/gh/*` isn't in the deny list, but the sandbox read restrictions would need checking).
downside is a plaintext storage and, if i'm not mistaken, claude has to read the token

Actually, Claude doesn't read the token. The flow is:

1. Claude runs `git push`
2. Git spawns `gh auth git-credential` as a subprocess
3. `gh` reads the token from `hosts.yml` internally
4. `gh` passes it to git via subprocess pipe
5. Git authenticates

The token never enters Claude's context window — it stays inside the subprocess communication between git and gh. Claude just sees "push succeeded" or "push failed."

The plaintext storage concern is real though. Your GitHub token sits in `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml` as readable text, protected only by `chmod 600`. Same security model as SSH private keys, but still a step down from keychain.

okay, not the worst-- but it feels irresponsible as a developer to leave my key even moderately exposed if people are git pulling from my repo and trusting me. if i expose myself to attack, they're in the line of fire too

That's the right instinct. Your threat model isn't just "what can happen to me" — it's "what can happen through me." A compromised GitHub token means someone pushes to your repos, and anyone pulling trusts that code.

The counterargument: your SSH private key has the same exposure model (plaintext file, filesystem permissions) and you've been trusting that for years. But I take your point — adding *another* plaintext credential when you're specifically building a security-conscious tool sends the wrong signal.

I think this is actually two separate problems worth distinguishing:

1. **Your workflow** — can you live with manual `git push` in a separate terminal? It's one command, already in muscle memory. Everything else (commits, staging, diffs, branch management) works inside Claude Code. The friction is real but narrow.

2. **Weft users** — the safer-yolo guide doesn't depend on solving this. The guide is about sandbox + deny rules + hooks + gitleaks. Git auth is orthogonal — users bring their own credential setup. Some will have SSH working, some will already use insecure storage, some will push manually. The guide doesn't promise automated push; it promises safe bypass mode.

The day's work produced a publishable security guide, a thorough threat model, and a tested layered configuration. The git push issue is a genuine limitation, but it's not what the guide is about.

maybe i've gotten discouraged because this was the very first thing to happen after switching to bypass permissions mode. it feels like this speedbump predicts a multitude of others to come, and it's not necessarily rational to predict that or give up at this phase. running git push a few times a day is not the worst -- but what claude is very good at is helping to manage many other git actions that i'm concerned will be forfeited (resolving merge conflicts, handling branch juggling when multiple categories of change are in effect, etc).

That's worth separating out. The credential issue blocks exactly one operation: anything that talks to the remote (push, pull, fetch). Everything local is unaffected.

So merge conflicts, branch creation/switching, rebasing, cherry-picking, stashing, diffing, log inspection, staging specific files — all of that works inside the sandbox. That's where the actual complexity lives, and where Claude saves you the most time.

The commands you'd run manually:

- `git push`
- `git pull` / `git fetch`

That's it. And you're already in a terminal alongside Claude Code, so it's a tab switch, not a context switch.

The discouragement makes sense — you hit a wall on the first action and your nervous system generalized it to "this whole approach is broken." But the evidence says the opposite: the one thing that doesn't work is the thing that requires external authentication. Everything else the sandbox touches is local filesystem and local git operations, which have no auth dependency.

ok i think you've talked me into persevering. thanks. i'll keep the manual git pull/push/fetch responsibility myself and see how the rest goes for a while. i do think i need to update the guide that's about to merge to the weft harness though. can you find the appropriate place in this version: /Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/weft/guides/safer-yolo-mode.md and recommend an addition that completes the picture?

items.annotation: Full unblocking arc: user worried about security implications of workaround, then generalizes the discouragement ('this speedbump predicts a multitude'), then Claude separates the specific blocker from the general case, user perseveres. Classic stuck-by-overgeneralization pattern.
items.inject_text: Add the 5a threat vector section under 'Known limitations' (see chunk 1) to document the git remote exfiltration attack chains — this directly addresses the vulnerability you observed. (ref: design/complete/2026-03-05-git-remote-exfil-deny-rules.md)
items.context_text: User updating weft guide for git remote exfiltration fix. Needs to add 5a threat vectors under 'Known limitations' section. Local git operations (merge, branch) work inside sandbox; only remote auth fails.

transcript.content: [tools: Glob x2, Read x1]

**No, it doesn't.** The skill only has access to the **current session's** conversation history.

Looking at Phase 1, Step 3 ([SKILL.md:33-68](../../../documents/github/weftclone/weft/.claude/skills/session-review/SKILL.md#L33-L68)), the five evidence sources are:

1. **Conversation context** (line 35-36) — explicitly scoped to "the current session's full conversation history"
2. **Git history** since last review — commits and diffs
3. **Artifacts produced** — new/modified files
4. **Prior session logs** — frontmatter from earlier reviews
5. **Lesson scaffolds** — scaffold files in the window

Sources 2-5 are proxies for what happened in other sessions, but they're indirect. Git history shows *what* was built, not the conversation around it — the questions asked, explanations given, mistakes made, or debugging approaches tried. Those are where the richest learning signal lives, and they're only available for the session you're currently in.

This is a fundamental constraint of Claude Code's architecture: each session is isolated. There's no transcript store that a future session can read back. The skill works around this with git evidence and artifact inspection, but any conversation that didn't produce commits or get captured in a session log is invisible to future reviews.
[tools: Task x1]
Found it. Claude Code stores everything under `~/.claude/projects/`, with a `sessions-index.json` per project that has timestamps. Let me query those for today's conversations.
[tools: Bash x1]
No matches — the index files might be stale or use different date formats. Let me check what's actually in them and look at the raw JSONL files too.
[tools: Bash x2]
The index files are stale, but the actual JSONL files tell the truth. Let me get a clean list of today's conversations by reading the first line of each session file modified today.
[tools: Bash x2]

Here's the full picture. Filtering to sessions that were actually **active on 2026-02-28** (based on timestamps, not just file modification):

| # | Project | Session ID | Time (UTC) | Msgs | First prompt (summary) |
|---|---------|-----------|------------|------|----------------------|
| 1 | roger | `d9c06cac` | 14:15–16:10 | 694 | Presentation brainstorming & slides |
| 2 | roger | `a183fa0d` | 16:19–16:22 | 32 | Presentation slides (continued) |
| 3 | weft-dev | `718ea01a` | Feb 27 22:40–Feb 28 00:12 | 358 | Teacher role design, teaching principles |
| 4 | weft-dev | `820acbd2` | 00:21–01:56 | 178 | Teacher role docs, commit/push/PR, day recap |
| 5 | weft-dev | `6b4aaf80` | 00:54–01:25 | 88 | Startwork skill / coordination |
| 6 | **weft-dev** | `575580bc` | **16:20–now** | 40 | **This session** (session-review analysis) |

Six sessions total today, across two projects (`roger` and `weft-dev`). The remaining 11 files that showed up in the `find` had timestamps entirely on Feb 27 — they appeared because macOS updated file metadata today (likely from index rebuilds or the explore subagent reading them).

**Key takeaway for the session-review skill:** The data is there. Each session's JSONL at `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-project-path>/<session-uuid>.jsonl` contains the full conversation transcript with ISO timestamps. A review window could be implemented by:
1. Scanning all `sessions-index.json` files (or falling back to reading first/last lines of JSONL files when indices are stale)
2. Filtering by timestamp range since last review
3. Dispatching sub-agents to analyze each prior session's JSONL

items.annotation: Procedural/conceptual gap: discovering Claude Code stores sessions as JSONL files on disk. Learning the filesystem structure of the tool being used daily.
items.context_text: User is examining session data retrieval mechanics. Sessions stored in ~/.claude/projects/ with JSONL transcripts. Retrieved chunks discuss session-review limitations, stale indices, and memory decay research - all relevant to current exploration of session data accessibility.

transcript.content: testing out if permissions are working. can you attempt a git push
This is a risky action (pushing to remote affects shared state). Let me attempt it so you can see how the permission system responds.
[tools: ToolSearch x1, Bash x1]

The push was attempted but failed — not a permissions issue on the Claude Code side (I was allowed to run it), but a git credential issue. The remote is configured with HTTPS and there's no credential helper available in this environment to authenticate.

If you were testing whether Claude Code's permission system would prompt you before the push: it did allow the command through. If you want to test the hook/permission blocking specifically, you may need to adjust your settings. Want me to check your current hook or permission configuration?
no, this is a gitconfig issue. i've updated the auth settings in my gitconfig, try another push
[tools: Bash x1]

Progress — it's now using SSH instead of HTTPS, but the sandbox is blocking read access to `~/.ssh/known_hosts`. That's a sandbox filesystem restriction, not a git or SSH config issue. You'd need to allow read access to `~/.ssh/` in your sandbox settings for SSH-based git operations to work from within Claude Code.
can you try gh auth status?
[tools: Bash x1]

The `gh` CLI token is invalid. You'll need to re-authenticate outside of Claude Code:

```
gh auth login -h github.com
```

That's an interactive flow so it needs to run in your terminal directly.

items.annotation: Three git push attempts, each failing for a different reason (HTTPS credentials, SSH sandbox block, invalid gh token). Claude correctly diagnoses each failure but each suggested fix leads to the next wall. The pattern is visible in the text: user says try push, Claude reports failure and suggests fix, user applies fix, push fails again for a new reason. Diagnostic circling without convergence.
items.inject_text: Your push failures are sandbox-restricted (SSH file access blocked), not permission system issues. The sandbox blocks `~/.ssh/` access — you need sandbox permissions to test SSH-based Git operations. (ref: design/complete/2026-03-05-git-remote-exfil-deny-rules.md)
items.context_text: User testing Git permissions but push failures are sandbox-restricted (SSH file access blocked). Needs sandbox permissions for SSH Git tests.
